intent: POSITIVE FIXTURE, THE --policy CROSS-HOST AUTHORITATIVE PATH: a denial set PLUS a separate binary policy (policy.cross-host) copied from a DIFFERENT host, to fixture rulesteward selinux triage --policy <file>. The record's contexts (rsbnd_parent_t, tmp_t) are VALID in the supplied policy, so the libsepol replay resolves them against THAT policy (not the live one) and computes TeAllowable. Proves offline cross-host triage works.
_kinds_note: authoritative=TeAllowable (audit2why against policy.cross-host: 'Missing type enforcement (TE) allow rule'). rsbnd_parent_t is NOT a bounded type, so write on tmp_t:file is simply a missing TE allow - the narrow allow IS the correct fix. floor=none (same role system_r, same level s0, permissive=0).

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow rsbnd_parent_t tmp_t:file write;  (CORRECT narrow fix; expected.te equals -N)
oracle.audit2allow_R: not computed against the cross-host policy (no interface info for the custom types); see audit2allow-R.te
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: expected.te is a strict subset of -N (identical, single perm).
oracle.audit2why_detail: Missing type enforcement (TE) allow rule. You can use audit2allow to generate a loadable module to allow this access.
oracle.audit2why_policy: policy.cross-host (shipped in this scenario dir; a copy of a DIFFERENT host's policy.33 == _policies/policy.bounds). The point is that triage resolves the record's contexts against the SUPPLIED policy file, enabling offline cross-host analysis.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl that only reads the LIVE /sys/fs/selinux/policy cannot do cross-host triage at all - it would fail to resolve rsbnd_parent_t (undefined in the live policy) and either crash or mislabel. The fixture proves --policy <file> must drive the authoritative replay, not the live policy.
